note_tweet.text:
Earlier today, 0xcd2 deployed ~$2M into @apyx_fi PT-apyUSD across 8 buys at an effective fixed yield of 18.94% APY.

• Accumulated 2,034,423 PT-apyUSD in a single session
• Now the 3rd largest individual PT-apyUSD holder
• 18.94% Fixed APY for ~36 days
• $34,676 profit at maturity (1.73% ROI)

$STRC pays a floating monthly dividend of 11.5%, which in itself is already a compelling yield product.

apyUSD takes that underlying yield and lets you fix it on Pendle, locking in a rate that currently sits even higher than the floating rate.

The current implied APY is 19.3%, meaning you can enter today and outperform this whale's entry.

Pool liquidity is also decently deep, capable of absorbing a ~$37M swap at 18.88% effective fixed yield.

So yes, we can take your size (cc @saylor)

note_tweet.text:
Let's break it down for you.

Every apxUSD is overcollateralized by $STRC. But dividends are only emitted to a smaller subset of staked apxUSD (i.e. apyUSD), which is why apyUSD yield is typically higher than $STRC's dividend rate.

Pendle is a yield infra that splits these apyUSD into principal and yield, which allow them to be traded separately ala bond stripping if you're familiar with that.

The principal portion is essentially "zero coupon bond" for apyUSD.

Factor in APYX points as well in the "yield" portion, which represent their upcoming airdrop, and the fact that apyUSD naturally already receives outsized $STRC dividend rate, is why the fixed yield portion on Pendle is priced higher.

This is just one asset. Every asset is well documented in their respective market page, feel free to browse, Brad.

data.text:
Royco is quietly becoming core infrastructure for STRC yield onchain, a credit vault that dynamically prices risk between tranches, and now Pendle sits on top of that stack as a yield trading/fixed yield layer.

P.S. srRoyAPYUSD on Pendle soon 😉

jrRoyAPYUSD: https://t.co/yv24hxVnyJ

TL;DR

jrRoyAPYUSD → Amplified yield, first-loss seat. You earn the premium + absorb the drawdown (if any) https://t.co/GSKBV63H3A

When apyUSD drawdowns, the pool doesn't immediately crystallize a loss.

It opens a 30-day Observation Period instead. Senior withdrawals pause. Yield flows entirely to Junior.

Recover within the window → Nothing happens

Don't recover → jrRoyAPYUSD takes the hit

The yields aren't static and are priced dynamically based on Utilization.

Utilization tracks how stretched Junior's protective capacity is. Think of it like a borrow rate curve.

Past 90%, the risk premium Senior pays Junior steepens sharply to attract more Junior capital.

Any

note_tweet.text:
The yields aren't static and are priced dynamically based on Utilization.

Utilization tracks how stretched Junior's protective capacity is. Think of it like a borrow rate curve.

Past 90%, the risk premium Senior pays Junior steepens sharply to attract more Junior capital.

Any excess Junior capital above the minimum coverage (15%) can serve as liquidity for Junior depositors to exit.

Current utilization: 93%, so Junior is earning an elevated premium right now.

data.text:
jrRoyAPYUSD captures a premium paid by Seniors for drawdown protection, earning the underlying rate plus a premium for the risk they take on.

In return, you'll also be the first line of defense when apyUSD takes a hit.
Current APY: 30.42% (7D avg: 19.31%) https://t.co/CHd6cwo6WM

One key number to watch 👇🏻

Coverage Ratio = Total vault / Senior vault.

The minimum safe threshold is 107.5%. Below that, srUSDat minting and jrUSDat redemption are temporarily halted until the threshold recovers.

Below 100% is when jrUSDat is wiped out and when srUSDat starts

data.text:
Since the Senior tranche is typically much larger, that leftover pile divided across a small Junior base produces amplified yield - typically ~25% APR.

Do note however that jrUSDat will also be the first to absorb losses when STRC's price drops.

srUSDat earns 65% of STRC's dividend yield. Yield floats with whatever STRC pays

e.g. 11.5% APR x 65% = ~7.5% APR

In other words, your yield is lower, but your principal is insulated from STRC price swings.

jrUSDat earns everything left over (i.e. 35%) after the Senior is

data.text:

STRC pays a dividend. That dividend is split between two tranches: Senior (srUSDat) and Junior (jrUSDat).

Senior gets paid first, and Junior gets the rest. https://t.co/sM80Sgshd3

note_tweet.text:
Lots of people are wondering how it's possible for @apyx_fi PT-apyUSD to pay ~16% Fixed APY, more than @saylor's STRC dividend rate of 11.5%.

ELI5 👇🏻
> apxUSD is backed by STRC and SATA, but dividends only flow to apyUSD
> apyUSD = staked apxUSD
> As long as <100% of apxUSD is staked, apyUSD earns a disproportionately larger share of dividends

The Pendle story:
> Pendle is a yield tokenization protocol, similar to bond stripping
> apyUSD's yield is stripped so it can be traded separately
> The principal portion becomes PT, essentially a zero coupon bond
> But don't forget - apyUSD comes with APYX points too, representing their impending airdrop
> Disproportionate dividends + airdrop upside, both priced in

This is why PT-apyUSD is 16% Fixed APY 👍🏻

TL;DR PT-apyUSD earns more than STRC because it concentrates dividends across fewer stakers. Pendle then lets you lock that rate in at a fixed return.
